The Power of God Fully Competent to Effect the Resurrection of the Flesh.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: The Power of God Fully Competent to Effect the Resurrection of the Flesh.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Thus far touching my eulogy of the flesh, in opposition to its enemies, who are, notwithstanding, its greatest friends also; for there is nobody who lives so much in accordance with the flesh as they who deny the resurrection of the flesh, inasmuch as they despise all its discipline, while they disbelieve its punishment.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: It is a shrewd saying which the Paraclete utters concerning these persons by the mouth of the prophetess Prisca: “They are carnal, and yet they hate the flesh.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Since, then, the flesh has the best guarantee that could possibly accrue for securing to it the recompense of salvation, ought we not also to consider well the power, and might, and competency of God Himself, whether He be so great as to be able to rebuild and restore the edifice of the flesh, which had become dilapidated and blocked up, and in every possible way dislocated?—whether He has promulgated in the public domains of nature any analogies to convince us of His power in this respect, lest any should happen to be still thirsting for the knowledge of God, when faith in Him must rest on no other basis than the belief that He is able to do all things?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: You have, no doubt amongst your philosophers men who maintain that this world is without a beginning or a maker.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: It is, however, much more true, that nearly all the heresies allow it an origin and a maker, and ascribe its creation to our God.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Firmly believe, therefore, that He produced it wholly out of nothing, and then you have found the knowledge of God, by believing that He possesses such mighty power.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But some persons are too weak to believe all this at first, owing to their views about Matter.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: They will rather have it, after the philosophers, that the universe was in the beginning made by God out of underlying matter.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, even if this opinion could be held in truth, since He must be acknowledged to have produced in His reformation of matter far different substances and far different forms from those which Matter itself possessed, I should maintain, with no less persistence, that He produced these things out of nothing, since they absolutely had no existence at all previous to His production of them.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, where is the difference between a thing’s being produced out of nothing or out of something, if so be that what existed not comes into being, when even to have had no existence is tantamount to having been nothing?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The contrary is likewise true; for having once existed amounts to having been something.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If, however, there is a difference, both alternatives support my position.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For if God produced all things whatever out of nothing, He will be able to draw forth from nothing even the flesh which had fallen into nothing; or if He moulded other things out of matter, He will be able to call forth the flesh too from somewhere else, into whatever abyss it may have been engulphed.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And surely He is most competent to re-create who created, inasmuch as it is a far greater work to have produced than to have reproduced, to have imparted a beginning, than to have maintained a continuance.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: On this principle, you may be quite sure that the restoration of the flesh is easier than its first formation.
